why: Primary orchestration brain, planner, router, policy engine, and state layer.
install_method: standalone canonical repo with internal routing and orchestration modules
integration_steps: Create canonical repo registry and routing manifest inside archonx-os.
Add worker adapters for Darya, Agent Zero, Goose, and agency-agents.
Enforce controller-worker separation with job contracts and audit logs.
Use mcp2cli for all MCP and OpenAPI invocation paths.
risks_if_misplaced: No single source of truth for routing

why: Primary control tower for local machine operations, file access, process control, and execution visibility.
install_method: run beside archonx-os as a privileged workstation/server sidecar
integration_steps: Treat DesktopCommanderMCP as the default execution gateway for local operations.
Expose capability metadata to archonx-os.
Wrap high-token tool calls through mcp2cli.
Add approval boundaries for destructive actions.
risks_if_misplaced: Desktop control gets duplicated in worker repos
Weak audit boundary for machine actions

why: Mandatory token-saving invocation layer for MCP and OpenAPI tools.
install_method: sidecar service with adapters registered by archonx-os
integration_steps: Place mcp2cli on every tool invocation path leaving archonx-os.
Create canonical aliases for common operations.
Log raw-to-compact invocation mappings.
Use it as the standard adapter for Goose and worker agents.

why: Mandatory multi-agent execution layer that should sit under Archon control.
install_method: worker service registered to archonx-os job router
integration_steps: Treat agency-agents as a worker cluster, not the top-level controller.
Define task envelope schema from Archon.
Route tool usage through mcp2cli and DesktopCommanderMCP.
Attach run telemetry back to archonx-os state.

why: Execution hands layer for coding and task execution under Archon planning control.
install_method: worker service with task API contract controlled by archonx-os
integration_steps: Keep Darya behind Archon routing and policy checks.
Require job envelopes, budget limits, and result schemas.
Use DesktopCommanderMCP for local machine access.
Store execution traces in Archon state.
risks_if_misplaced: Execution layer starts acting as planner

why: Specialized worker agent layer for delegated tasks, not the central orchestrator.
integration_steps: Register as a worker class in the Archon router.
Constrain it to specific job types and tool budgets.
Return normalized results to archonx-os.
risks_if_misplaced: Overlapping role with Darya and agency-agents

why: Cloud coding workflow capability that should operate as an elastic remote coding worker under Archon supervision.
install_method: cloud worker integration with extension manifest and repo inventory feed
integration_steps: Feed Goose the canonical repo inventory and task taxonomy from archonx-os.
Attach extension metadata and allowed tool bundles.
Route external tool access through mcp2cli where possible.
risks_if_misplaced: Cloud coding becomes blind to ecosystem structure

why: Plugin and app adapter layer for external services and product-specific integrations.
install_method: plugin package loaded by archonx-os integration layer
integration_steps: Define plugin manifest contract.
Map each external connector to env categories rather than raw secrets.
Make plugin routing discoverable in Archon registry.
risks_if_misplaced: External integrations leak into core brain code

why: Specialized execution-focused component in the seed graph; best fit is under Archon as a worker.
integration_steps: Define its narrow execution specialty.
Attach to Archon routing rules and capability registry.
Share invocation layer with mcp2cli.

why: Better fit as an MCP tool provider than as a core controller.
install_method: MCP plugin registered through mcp2cli and archonx-os
integration_steps: Register tool manifest and capability tags.
Expose through mcp2cli to reduce token overhead.
Limit direct agent-to-plugin coupling.
risks_if_misplaced: Direct coupling between workers and tools

why: Network edge exposure should be isolated from application logic and run as infrastructure sidecar.
install_method: infra sidecar managed by archonx-os deployment policies
integration_steps: Centralize tunnel mappings in Archon deployment manifests.
Do not bury edge exposure in app repos.
Use per-product routes for white-label separation.

why: Second-brain and operational memory stack for Pauli.
install_method: integrated external memory service referenced by archonx-os and Pauli layers
integration_steps: Define canonical database and page schemas.
Sync Archon memory writes and Pauli funnel reads against that schema.
Keep Notion integration behind service adapters.
risks_if_misplaced: Pauli funnel disconnected from source memory

why: Canonical public-funnel/frontend paired with Notion as the second-brain backend.
install_method: product frontend wired to Archon memory and orchestration services
integration_steps: Make it the canonical Pauli frontend experience.
Pull structured content from Notion-backed memory through Archon APIs.
Separate product UX concerns from orchestration logic.
risks_if_misplaced: Pauli knowledge and public funnel drift apart

why: Product and brand layer on top of Archon-X, powered by the Pauli memory and funnel stack.
install_method: brand layer built on top of shared Archon services
integration_steps: Treat The Pauli Effect as a product layer, not the core OS.
Use shared Archon service contracts underneath.
Keep brand-specific UX and content isolated from core orchestration code.

why: Phase 1 found many Pauli references but not a stable canonical per-repo inventory.
install_method: family-level inventory and consolidation under Archon repo registry
integration_steps: Create a canonical Pauli repo map.
Merge duplicate content repos into memory, frontend, and product buckets.
Archive anything not used by the canonical Pauli stack.
